pi_items.metadata_value: Falls, Struck-By Objects, Electrocution, and Caught-In/Between hazards
pi_items.verified_value: The Fatal Four are: (1) Falls, (2) Struck-By, (3) Electrocution, (4) Caught-In or -Between. These are the four leading causes of construction worker deaths recognized by OSHA.
pi_items.source: OSHA official sources and search results confirming Fatal Four concept is a real federal safety initiative

pi_items.metadata_value: More than 36 percent of all fatalities in construction
pi_items.verified_value: Falls account for more than 36% of all fatalities in the construction industry; this is documented as exceeding 36% in recent years, with some sources citing 37% in 2021.
pi_items.source: OSHA Texas (tdi.texas.gov) and construction safety statistics sources

pi_items.metadata_value: The fatal four have been responsible for over 60% of all construction worker fatalities
pi_items.verified_value: An astounding 60% of construction worker deaths can be attributed to OSHA's Fatal Four. Recent data shows 65.5% from 2011-2021 analysis.
pi_items.source: OSHA official sources and construction safety statistics (CPWR, safety analytics)

pi_items.verified_value: Standard 1926.501 is titled 'Duty to have fall protection' and establishes employer requirements for implementing fall protection systems when employees work 6 feet or more above lower levels.

pi_items.metadata_value: Electrocution hazards covered under 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K standards
pi_items.verified_value: Electrical safety standards are contained in 29 CFR 1926 Subpart K, with key standards including 1926.403 (General requirements), 1926.416 (General requirements for power systems), and 1926.417 (Lockout and tagging).
pi_items.source: OSHA official Subpart K electrical standards and construction electrical incidents eTools

pi_items.metadata_value: Caught-In/Between hazards covered under excavation standards
pi_items.verified_value: Caught-in/between hazards in excavation are covered under 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P (Excavations), particularly 1926.651 (Specific Excavation Requirements) which addresses cave-ins, struck-by equipment, and other excavation hazards.
pi_items.source: OSHA official excavation standards and Subpart P regulations

pi_items.metadata_value: Struck-By hazards are covered under multiple 1926 standards
pi_items.verified_value: While there is no single standard for struck-by hazards, they are addressed across multiple standards including 1926 Subpart H (Materials Handling Storage Use and Disposal), 1926.250 (material storage), 1926.602 (material handling equipment), 1926.502(j) (falling object protection), and 1926.1424 (work area control).
pi_items.source: OSHA official struck-by eTool and multiple construction standards

pi_items.verified_value: The pattern 1926\d{3} correctly matches main OSHA construction standards such as 1926.501 (falls), 1926.502 (fall systems), 1926.651 (excavation), 1926.403 (electrical), etc.
pi_items.source: OSHA official Table of Contents for 29 CFR 1926 and specific standard pages

pi_items.metadata_value: OSHA publishes Fact Sheets and Quick Cards in PDF format
pi_items.verified_value: OSHA offers free Quick Cards (e.g., OSHA 3216 - Construction Hazards QuickCard) and Fact Sheets available as downloadable PDFs. Example: OSHA publication 3216 is the 'Construction Hazards (Top Four) QuickCard' available in English and Spanish.
pi_items.source: OSHA publications page (osha.gov/publications/bytype/quickcards) and PDF downloads

pi_items.verified_value: The official Occupational Safety and Health Administration website is osha.gov, which contains all federal construction standards (29 CFR 1926), publications, and guidance documents.
pi_items.source: OSHA official website and official federal sources

privileged_info_summary: The Fatal Four hazards (Falls, Struck-By, Electrocution, Caught-In/Between) are real OSHA-recognized leading causes of construction worker deaths, collectively accounting for approximately 60% of all construction fatalities. Falls alone cause over 36% of construction deaths. These hazards are covered under 29 CFR 1926 construction standards: 1926.501 (fall protection), 1926 Subpart K (electrical), 1926 Subpart H and related standards (struck-by), and 1926.651 (excavation/caught-in). OSHA.gov is the official source for all federal safety standards, and OSHA publishes free Quick Cards and Fact Sheets as PDF documents on construction safety topics.
